content:
## Positional Encoding

In standard sequence modeling architectures like RNNs or CNNs, the inherent progression of the data naturally encodes the order of the sequence. However, attention-based models often process all tokens simultaneously. To compensate for this lack of recurrence and convolution, researchers introduce **Positional Encoding**. This is specific information injected directly into the model at the very beginning of its processing pipeline, ensuring that the architecture can still leverage the absolute and relative positions of the items within the input sequence [[object:edb38348-9a37-5e6f-9215-cfe0b9ac92d2]].

Because the model processes inputs in parallel rather than sequentially, it requires a way to distinguish identical tokens that appear in different locations (e.g., the word "bank" in the first position versus the fifth position). Positional encodings are vectors generated using a specific mathematical function (such as overlapping sine and cosine waves of varying frequencies). These vectors share the exact same dimensionality as the standard token embeddings. By performing a simple element-wise addition between the token embedding and its corresponding positional encoding vector, the model successfully injects sequential context into the input, allowing the attention mechanism to understand sequence order [[object:edb38348-9a37-5e6f-9215-cfe0b9ac92d2]].

### Try it
Imagine a simple 4-dimensional embedding space. You have a pre-trained token embedding for the word `"model"` represented by the vector `[0.20, -0.50, 0.80, 0.10]`. 

Assume this token appears at position 2 in an input sequence, and your deterministic Positional Encoding function generates the vector `[0.10, 0.30, -0.20, 0.00]` for position 2. 

Calculate the final 4-dimensional vector that will actually be fed into the attention mechanism for this specific instance of the word `"model"`.

Assume this token appears at position 2 in an input sequence, and your deterministic Positional Encoding function generates the vector `[0.10, 0.30, -0.20, 0.00]` for position 2. 

Calculate the final 4-dimensional vector that will actually be fed into the attention mechanism for this specific instance of the word `"model"`.
